title: Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998
chapters: Chapter 23 (1998) · Royal Assent 2 July 1998 · In Force 2 July 1999 · Amends ERA 1996 by inserting Part IVA (ss.43A–43L)
desc: The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 provides protection for workers who make qualifying disclosures of information in the public interest (whistleblowing). The Act operates by inserting Part IVA (sections 43A to 43L) into the Employment Rights Act 1996. It protects workers who disclose information about criminal offences, failures to comply with legal obligations, miscarriages of justice, dangers to health and safety, environmental damage, and deliberate concealment of any of these matters. Workers who make qualifying disclosures are protected from dismissal and detriment. Dismissal for making a protected disclosure is automatically unfair. The Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 introduced the requirement that disclosures must be made in the public interest. There is no qualifying period of employment required to bring a whistleblowing claim and compensation is uncapped.

sections.title: Meaning of "protected disclosure"
sections.summary: A protected disclosure is a qualifying disclosure made by a worker in accordance with any of sections 43C to 43H. This is the gateway provision — a disclosure must first be a qualifying disclosure (s.43B) and then be made through one of the prescribed channels to be protected.
sections.text: In this Act a "protected disclosure" means a qualifying disclosure (as defined by section 43B) which is made by a worker in accordance with any of sections 43C to 43H.

versions.description: Original provision inserted by PIDA 1998. Gateway definition unchanged since enactment.

leadingCases.name: Western Union Payment Services UK Ltd v Anastasiou [2014] UKEAT/0135/13
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the s.43A gateway requires two elements: (1) the disclosure must be a qualifying disclosure under s.43B, and (2) it must be made through one of the channels prescribed by ss.43C-43H. Both conditions must be satisfied.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the two-stage test for protected disclosure status.

sections.title: Disclosures qualifying for protection
sections.summary: A qualifying disclosure is a disclosure of information which, in the reasonable belief of the worker making it, is made in the public interest and tends to show one or more of six specified types of wrongdoing: criminal offences, failure to comply with legal obligations, miscarriages of justice, danger to health and safety, damage to the environment, or deliberate concealment of information relating to any of these matters. The public interest requirement was added by the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013.
sections.text:
(1) In this Part a "qualifying disclosure" means any disclosure of information which, in the reasonable belief of the worker making the disclosure, is made in the public interest and tends to show one or more of the following—
(a) that a criminal offence has been committed, is being committed or is likely to be committed,
(b) that a person has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to comply with any legal obligation to which he is subject,
(c) that a miscarriage of justice has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur,
(d) that the health or safety of any individual has been, is being or is likely to be endangered,
(e) that the environment has been, is being or is likely to be damaged, or
(f) that information tending to show any matter falling within any one of the preceding paragraphs has been, is being or is likely to be deliberately concealed.

(2) For the purposes of subsection (1), it is immaterial whether the relevant failure occurred, occurs or would occur in the United Kingdom or elsewhere, and whether the law applying to it is that of the United Kingdom or of any other country or territory.

(3) A disclosure of information is not a qualifying disclosure if the person making the disclosure commits an offence by making it.

(4) A disclosure of information in respect of which a claim to legal professional privilege (or, in Scotland, to confidentiality as between client and professional legal adviser) could be maintained in legal proceedings is not a qualifying disclosure if it is made by a person to whom the information had been disclosed in the course of obtaining legal advice.

versions.description: Original enacted provision. No public interest requirement — any qualifying disclosure was sufficient. This allowed personal employment grievances to qualify.

Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.17 inserted the public interest test: disclosure must be made "in the public interest" in the reasonable belief of the worker. Removed good faith requirement as a condition for protection (good faith now only affects remedies). Overturned Parkins v Sodexho Ltd [2002] which had allowed purely personal grievances to qualify.

leadingCases.name: Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that a disclosure can be in the public interest even if the worker has a personal interest in the matter. The tribunal must consider all the circumstances including the number of people affected, the nature of the interests affected, and whether the wrongdoing involves a breach of a legal obligation.
leadingCases.significance: Leading authority on the meaning of 'public interest' in the whistleblowing context post-2013 amendment

leadingCases.name: Cavendish Munro Professional Risks Management Ltd v Geduld [2010] ICR 325
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT drew a distinction between conveying information and making an allegation. A qualifying disclosure must convey facts, not merely make an allegation or state a position. However, this rigid distinction was subsequently relaxed.
leadingCases.significance: Distinguished information from allegation in whistleblowing disclosures, though the rigid distinction has since been moderated

leadingCases.name: Kilraine v London Borough of Wandsworth [2018] EWCA Civ 1436
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the distinction between information and allegation is not a rigid dichotomy. A disclosure may contain sufficient factual content and specificity to amount to a disclosure of information even if it also contains an allegation.
leadingCases.significance: Relaxed the rigid information/allegation distinction from Cavendish Munro

refs: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.17 (public interest amendment)
Parkins v Sodexho Ltd [2002] IRLR 109 (overturned by 2013 amendment)

sections.title: Disclosure to employer or other responsible person
sections.summary: A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if the worker makes it to their employer, or where the worker reasonably believes that the relevant failure relates solely or mainly to the conduct of a person other than the employer, to that other person. This is the primary and most common disclosure route.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if the worker makes the disclosure in good faith—
(a) to his employer, or
(b) where the worker reasonably believes that the relevant failure relates solely or mainly to—
(i) the conduct of a person other than his employer, or
(ii) any other matter for which a person other than his employer has legal responsibility,
to that other person.

(2) A worker who, in accordance with a procedure whose use by him is authorised by his employer, makes a qualifying disclosure to a person other than his employer, is to be treated for the purposes of this Part as making the qualifying disclosure to his employer.

versions.description: Original provision. Required disclosure to be made 'in good faith' as a condition of protection.

Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.18 removed good faith as a condition of protection for s.43C disclosures. Good faith now only affects remedies (s.49(6A) — 25% reduction if not in good faith).
versions.amendedBy: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 (c.24) s.18

leadingCases.name: Parsons v Airplus International Ltd [2017] UKEAT/0111/17
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered what constitutes a disclosure 'to the employer'. A disclosure made to a manager in the course of their employment is a disclosure to the employer. The worker need not address the disclosure to a specific designated person.
leadingCases.significance: Broadened the scope of s.43C by confirming disclosure to any manager is disclosure to the employer.

sections.title: Disclosure to prescribed person
sections.summary: A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if the worker makes it to a person prescribed by the Secretary of State and reasonably believes that the relevant failure falls within the description of matters for which that person is prescribed, and that the information and any allegation contained in it are substantially true. Prescribed persons include regulators such as the HSE, CQC, FCA, ICO, and HMRC.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if the worker—
(a) makes the qualifying disclosure to a person prescribed by an order made by the Secretary of State for the purposes of this section, and
(b) reasonably believes—
(i) that the relevant failure falls within any description of matters in respect of which that person is so prescribed, and
(ii) that the information disclosed, and any allegation contained in it, are substantially true.

versions.description: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.17 removed the good faith requirement as a condition for protection under this section. Good faith now only affects remedies (s.49(6A)).

leadingCases.name: Korashi v Abertawe Bro Morgannwg University Local Health Board [2012] IRLR 4
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the requirement that information disclosed to a prescribed person must be 'substantially true'. The test is whether the worker reasonably believed it to be substantially true at the time of disclosure.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the subjective reasonable belief standard for disclosures to prescribed persons.

refs: s.43G ERA 1996 (disclosure in other cases — wider disclosure)

sections.title: Disclosure in other cases
sections.summary: A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if the worker makes the disclosure in the reasonable belief that the information and any allegation are substantially true, does not make it for purposes of personal gain, and meets one of the preconditions (reasonable belief employer would subject them to detriment, no prescribed person exists, or evidence would be concealed or destroyed). The disclosure must also be reasonable in all the circumstances.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if—
(a) the worker reasonably believes that the information disclosed, and any allegation contained in it, are substantially true,
(b) he does not make the disclosure for purposes of personal gain,
(c) any of the conditions in subsection (2) is met, and
(d) in all the circumstances of the case, it is reasonable for him to make the disclosure.

(2) The conditions referred to in subsection (1)(c) are—
(a) that, at the time he makes the disclosure, the worker reasonably believes that he will be subjected to a detriment by his employer if he makes a disclosure to his employer or in accordance with section 43F,
(b) that, in a case where no person is prescribed for the purposes of section 43F in relation to the relevant failure, the worker reasonably believes that it is likely that evidence relating to the relevant failure will be concealed or destroyed if he makes a disclosure to his employer, or
(c) that the worker has previously made a disclosure of substantially the same information—
(i) to his employer, or
(ii) in accordance with section 43F.

(3) In determining for the purposes of subsection (1)(d) whether it is reasonable for the worker to make the disclosure, regard shall be had, in particular, to—
(a) the identity of the person to whom the disclosure is made,
(b) the seriousness of the relevant failure,
(c) whether the relevant failure is continuing or is likely to occur in the future,
(d) whether the disclosure is made in breach of a duty of confidentiality owed by the employer to any other person,
(e) in a case falling within subsection (2)(c)(i) or (ii), any action which the employer or the person to whom the previous disclosure in accordance with section 43F was made has taken or might reasonably be expected to have taken as a result of the previous disclosure, and
(f) in a case falling within subsection (2)(c)(i), whether in making the disclosure to the employer the worker complied with any procedure whose use by him was authorised by the employer.

versions.description: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.17 removed good faith from the preconditions for protection. Good faith now affects remedy (25% reduction in compensation if disclosure not made in good faith, per s.49(6A)).

leadingCases.name: Babula v Waltham Forest College [2007] EWCA Civ 174
leadingCases.heldText: A worker's reasonable belief that information tends to show a relevant failure is sufficient for a qualifying disclosure, even if the belief turns out to be wrong. The test is whether the worker had a reasonable belief, not whether the information actually disclosed a relevant failure.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority that reasonable belief is sufficient — the disclosure does not need to be objectively correct

refs: s.43H ERA 1996 (disclosure of exceptionally serious failure)
s.49(6A) ERA 1996 (good faith reduction in compensation)

sections.title: Disclosure of exceptionally serious failure
sections.summary: A qualifying disclosure of an exceptionally serious failure is made in accordance with this section if the worker reasonably believes the information and any allegation are substantially true, does not make it for personal gain, and the relevant failure is of an exceptionally serious nature. This is the broadest disclosure channel — it has no requirement to first disclose to the employer or prescribed person.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if—
(a) the worker reasonably believes that the information disclosed, and any allegation contained in it, are substantially true,
(b) he does not make the disclosure for purposes of personal gain,
(c) the relevant failure is of an exceptionally serious nature, and
(d) in all the circumstances of the case, it is reasonable for him to make the disclosure.

(2) In determining for the purposes of subsection (1)(d) whether it is reasonable for the worker to make the disclosure, regard shall be had, in particular, to the identity of the person to whom the disclosure is made.

versions.description: Original provision. Required disclosure to be made 'in good faith'.
ERRA 2013 s.18 removed good faith condition.

leadingCases.name: Darnton v University of Surrey [2003] ICR 615
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the test for 'exceptionally serious' failure under s.43H. The threshold is high — the failure must be of an exceptionally serious nature, going beyond a 'serious' failure. Examples cited in parliamentary debate included disasters, systematic fraud, and threats to life.
leadingCases.significance: Established the high threshold for 'exceptionally serious' wider disclosures.

refs: s.43G ERA 1996 (disclosure in other cases — compare less serious threshold)

sections.title: Contractual duties of confidentiality
sections.summary: Any provision in an agreement (including a contract of employment, settlement agreement, or non-disclosure agreement) is void insofar as it purports to preclude the worker from making a protected disclosure. This provision prevents employers from using NDAs or confidentiality clauses to prevent whistleblowing.
sections.text:
(1) Any provision in an agreement to which this section applies is void in so far as it purports to preclude the worker from making a protected disclosure.

(2) This section applies to any agreement between a worker and his employer (whether a worker's contract or not), including an agreement to refrain from instituting or continuing any proceedings under this Act or any proceedings for breach of contract.

versions.description: Original provision. Voiding of contractual confidentiality provisions insofar as they preclude protected disclosures. Unchanged since enactment.

leadingCases.name: Garamukanwa v United Kingdom [2019] (Application No. 70573/17)
leadingCases.heldText: The principle that contractual confidentiality provisions cannot override whistleblowing protection is consistent with ECHR Article 10 (freedom of expression). Employers cannot use NDAs to prevent protected disclosures.
leadingCases.significance: ECHR alignment with s.43J's voiding of confidentiality provisions that would prevent protected disclosure.

refs: s.43K ERA 1996 (extension of protection to other workers — Crown, NHS, police)
s.203 ERA 1996 (restrictions on contracting out — general)

sections.title: Protected disclosures: detriment
sections.summary: A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by their employer done on the ground that the worker has made a protected disclosure. Where the detriment is done by a fellow worker or agent of the employer, the employer is vicariously liable unless they took all reasonable steps to prevent it.
sections.text:
(1) A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by his employer done on the ground that the worker has made a protected disclosure.

(1A) A worker ("W") has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, done—
(a) by another worker of W's employer in the course of that other worker's employment, or
(b) by an agent of W's employer with the employer's authority,
on the ground that W has made a protected disclosure.

(1B) Where a worker is subjected to detriment by anything done as mentioned in subsection (1A), that thing is treated as also done by the worker's employer.

(1C) For the purposes of subsection (1B), it is immaterial whether the thing is done with the knowledge or approval of the worker's employer.

(1D) In proceedings against W's employer in respect of anything alleged to have been done as mentioned in subsection (1A)(a), it is a defence for the employer to show that the employer took all reasonable steps to prevent the other worker—
(a) from doing that thing, or
(b) from doing anything of that description.

(2) This section does not apply where—
(a) the worker is an employee, and
(b) the detriment in question amounts to dismissal (within the meaning of Part X).

versions.description: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 inserted subsections (1A)–(1E), establishing vicarious liability of the employer for detriment done by fellow workers or agents. This addressed the gap identified in cases where the detrimental act was done by an individual co-worker rather than the employer directly.

leadingCases.name: NHS Manchester v Fecitt [2012] ICR 372
leadingCases.heldText: The causation test for whistleblowing detriment is that the protected disclosure must be the reason, or if more than one the principal reason, for the detrimental treatment. This mirrors the test for whistleblowing dismissal under s.103A.
leadingCases.significance: Established the causation standard for whistleblowing detriment claims

refs: s.48(1A) ERA 1996 (complaints to tribunal about detriment)
s.103A ERA 1996 (unfair dismissal for protected disclosure)

sections.title: Protected disclosure: unfair dismissal
sections.summary: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason or the principal reason for the dismissal is that the employee made a protected disclosure. This is an automatically unfair dismissal — no qualifying period of continuous employment is required and the normal cap on compensation does not apply.
sections.text: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of this Part as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee made a protected disclosure.

versions.description: Original provision inserted by PIDA 1998. Automatically unfair dismissal for protected disclosure. No qualifying period. Uncapped compensation. Unchanged since enactment.

leadingCases.name: Kuzel v Roche Products Ltd [2008] EWCA Civ 380
leadingCases.heldText: Where the employee asserts that the reason for dismissal was a protected disclosure, the burden is on the employee to prove that the disclosure was the reason. If the employer's asserted reason is rejected, the tribunal is not bound to accept the employee's reason — but may draw an inference that the disclosure was the reason.
leadingCases.significance: Leading authority on the burden of proof in whistleblowing dismissal claims

leadingCases.name: Royal Mail Group Ltd v Jhuti [2019] UKSC 55
leadingCases.heldText: Where a line manager's reason for seeking the employee's dismissal was the protected disclosure, but the decision-maker was unaware of the disclosure, the reason for dismissal is the reason operated on by the person who had the real reason for the dismissal (the line manager), not the decision-maker. This is the 'hidden reason' principle.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority on attributing the hidden motive of a manipulating manager as the reason for dismissal

refs: s.108(3)(ff) ERA 1996 (no qualifying period for s.103A dismissal)
s.124(1A) ERA 1996 (no cap on compensation for s.103A dismissal)
s.49(6A) ERA 1996 (25% reduction if disclosure not made in good faith)

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 — strengthened whistleblower protections for sexual harassment disclosures (anticipated April 2026)
Government Review of Whistleblowing Framework — launched March 2023, ongoing
Proposed Office of the Whistleblower — discussed in Parliament but not yet legislated

parliamentaryOrigin.sponsoringMinister: Richard Shepherd MP (Conservative, Aldridge-Brownhills) — Private Members' Bill, with government support from Ian McCartney MP (Minister of State, DTI)

readings.keyPoints: Two earlier Private Members' Bills on whistleblower protection were introduced by Tony Wright MP (1995) and Don Touhig MP (1996) but both failed to complete their parliamentary passage. Richard Shepherd introduced his own version on 1 March 1996 during the Major government, which also failed. The incoming Blair government agreed to support Shepherd's re-introduced Bill in the 1997-98 session.
readings.significance: Three failed attempts preceded the successful 1997-98 Bill. The political context shifted when Labour came to power in May 1997 and agreed to support the Bill, on condition that it amend ERA 1996 rather than create a standalone statute.

readings.keyPoints: Richard Shepherd MP re-introduced the Public Interest Disclosure Bill as a Private Members' Bill in the first session of the new Labour Parliament. The government agreed to provide parliamentary time and drafting support through DTI Minister Ian McCartney.
readings.significance: A rare example of a government supporting an Opposition backbencher's Private Members' Bill. The cross-party nature of the Bill was fundamental to its success.

readings.keyPoints: Richard Shepherd presented the Bill. He stated: 'The Bill is, as its name implies, a public interest measure. Were it merely an employee rights measure, I doubt that I would be able to inform the committee that its objectives are supported by the Institute of Directors, the Confederation of British Industry and the Committee on Standards in Public Life.' The Bill had unprecedented cross-party and cross-industry support: CBI, IOD, TUC, medical, legal and accountancy professions. Shepherd cited the Zeebrugge ferry disaster, the Barlow Clowes financial fraud, and the Frank Beck child abuse case as examples where whistleblowers had raised alarms that were ignored.
readings.significance: The Second Reading debate established the Bill as a public interest measure, not merely an employee rights measure. Shepherd's statement that CBI, IOD, and the Nolan Committee all supported the Bill is admissible under Pepper v Hart to establish the legislative intent.

readings.keyPoints: Ian McCartney (Minister of State, DTI) worked closely with Shepherd to refine the Bill in Committee. The tiered disclosure framework was established: disclosure to employer (s.43C, lowest threshold), disclosure to prescribed regulator (s.43F, 'substantially true' requirement), wider disclosure (s.43G, multiple conditions), exceptionally serious failure (s.43H, broadest channel). The charity Public Concern at Work was centrally involved in the drafting and consultation.
readings.significance: The tiered disclosure framework — encouraging internal disclosure first, then to prescribed regulators, with wider disclosure only in specified circumstances — was a deliberate policy architecture designed to balance whistleblower protection with employer interests.

readings.keyPoints: Lord Borrie introduced the Bill in the Lords, paying tribute to Richard Shepherd and Ian McCartney. Lord Borrie noted the Bill was 'to a large extent, based on the work of a charity, Public Concern at Work'. Cross-party support continued in the Lords. The Bill was described as having passed the Commons 'with strong support from both the Government and the Opposition'.
readings.significance: Lord Borrie's tribute to Public Concern at Work (now Protect) established the charity's foundational role in the development of UK whistleblowing law.

readings.keyPoints: Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (c.23) received Royal Assent on 2 July 1998. The Act came into force on 2 July 1999. It operates by inserting Part IVA (ss.43A-43L) into the Employment Rights Act 1996.
readings.significance: The most significant whistleblower protection statute in the UK. The Act has been described as a model for whistleblower protection legislation internationally. Subsequently amended by the ERRA 2013 (public interest requirement, good faith shift to remedies) and by ERA 2025 (sexual harassment disclosure protections).

significantAmendments.text: The tiered disclosure framework was established: internal disclosure to employer with lowest threshold, disclosure to prescribed regulators with 'substantially true' requirement, wider disclosure with multiple preconditions, and exceptionally serious failure as the broadest channel.
significantAmendments.outcome: Enacted as ss.43C-43H ERA 1996. The framework reflects the principle that internal resolution should be encouraged, with external disclosure protected only where internal routes are exhausted or inappropriate.
significantAmendments.significance: The tiered architecture was Public Concern at Work's central recommendation. It balances the public interest in disclosure against the employer's legitimate interest in internal resolution.

significantAmendments.text: The government's condition for supporting the Bill was that it amend the Employment Rights Act 1996 rather than create a standalone whistleblowing statute.
significantAmendments.outcome: PIDA 1998 inserts Part IVA (ss.43A-43L) into ERA 1996. Whistleblowing protections are therefore embedded within the existing employment rights framework, with remedies through the employment tribunal system.
significantAmendments.significance: The decision to embed PIDA within ERA 1996 rather than create standalone legislation meant whistleblowing protection was integrated into the employment tribunal system, with its existing remedies, time limits, and enforcement mechanisms.

keyDebates.provision: General — Public interest measure, not merely employee rights
keyDebates.ministerialStatement: Richard Shepherd: 'The Bill is, as its name implies, a public interest measure. Were it merely an employee rights measure, I doubt that I would be able to inform the committee that its objectives are supported by the Institute of Directors, the Confederation of British Industry and the Committee on Standards in Public Life.' He cited the Zeebrugge disaster, Barlow Clowes, and the Frank Beck child abuse case as examples where whistleblowers raised alarms that were ignored with catastrophic consequences.
keyDebates.significance: Established the foundational principle that PIDA exists to protect disclosures in the public interest, not merely to protect employee grievances. This Pepper v Hart statement was central to the ERRA 2013 amendment introducing the public interest requirement.

keyDebates.ministerialStatement: Ian McCartney confirmed that the tiered structure was designed to encourage workers to raise concerns internally first, while ensuring that where internal disclosure is not possible or appropriate, external disclosure routes are available and protected. The framework 'balances the interests of the worker, the employer, and the public'.
keyDebates.significance: McCartney's statement establishes the policy intent behind the tiered disclosure channels — internal first, external where justified. This intent informs the interpretation of the reasonableness test in s.43G.

precedingDocuments.title: Committee on Standards in Public Life — First Report (Nolan Report)
precedingDocuments.publisher: Committee on Standards in Public Life (Lord Nolan, Chair)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Nolan Committee recommended that organisations should have confidential procedures for raising concerns about wrongdoing internally. The Committee's support for the Bill was cited by Richard Shepherd at Second Reading.

precedingDocuments.title: Public Concern at Work — Policy Development
precedingDocuments.significance: The charity Public Concern at Work (founded 1993, now Protect) was centrally involved in drafting and consulting on the Bill. Lord Borrie described the Act as 'to a large extent, based on the work of' the charity. PCaW developed the tiered disclosure framework.

precedingDocuments.title: Zeebrugge Ferry Disaster — Sheen Inquiry Report
precedingDocuments.significance: The Herald of Free Enterprise capsized on 6 March 1987, killing 193 people. The Sheen Inquiry found that crew members had raised safety concerns that were ignored. Cited by Richard Shepherd at Second Reading as an example of the catastrophic consequences of ignoring whistleblowers.

precedingDocuments.title: Graham Pink Case
precedingDocuments.significance: Graham Pink, a charge nurse at Stepping Hill Hospital, raised concerns about unsafe staffing levels on night shifts. He was dismissed after taking his concerns public. His case generated public support for whistleblower protection legislation.

explanatoryNotes.note: Explanatory Notes were published alongside the Act. They explain each section's purpose and the tiered disclosure framework architecture.

leadingCases.name: Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979
leadingCases.heldText: A disclosure can be in the public interest even if the worker has a personal interest. The tribunal must consider all circumstances including the number of people affected and the nature of the wrongdoing.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the 'public interest' requirement post-ERRA 2013.

leadingCases.name: Royal Mail Group Ltd v Jhuti [2019] UKSC 55
leadingCases.heldText: Where a line manager's hidden reason for seeking dismissal was the protected disclosure, that is the reason for dismissal — even if the decision-maker was unaware.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority on the 'hidden reason' principle in whistleblowing dismissals.

leadingCases.name: Kilraine v London Borough of Wandsworth [2018] EWCA Civ 1436
leadingCases.heldText: The distinction between information and allegation is not a rigid dichotomy. A disclosure with sufficient factual content and specificity is a disclosure of information even if it also contains an allegation.
leadingCases.significance: Relaxed the information/allegation distinction, lowering the threshold for qualifying disclosures.

leadingCases.name: Kuzel v Roche Products Ltd [2008] EWCA Civ 380
leadingCases.heldText: The burden is on the employee to prove the protected disclosure was the reason for dismissal. If the employer's asserted reason is rejected, the tribunal may draw an inference.
leadingCases.significance: Leading authority on the burden of proof in whistleblowing dismissal claims.

leadingCases.name: NHS Manchester v Fecitt [2012] ICR 372
leadingCases.heldText: The protected disclosure must be the reason, or the principal reason, for the detrimental treatment.
leadingCases.significance: Established the causation standard for whistleblowing detriment claims.

leadingCases.name: Babula v Waltham Forest College [2007] EWCA Civ 174
leadingCases.heldText: Reasonable belief that information tends to show a relevant failure is sufficient — the belief does not need to be objectively correct.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the subjective reasonable belief standard for qualifying disclosures.

leadingCases.name: Cavendish Munro v Geduld [2010] ICR 325
leadingCases.heldText: Drew a distinction between conveying information (protected) and making an allegation (not protected). Subsequently moderated by Kilraine.
leadingCases.significance: Established the information/allegation distinction — subsequently relaxed but not overruled.
